content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Why your rule-based systems might be your biggest bottleneck — and how intelligent agents can set you free.
I built a solution to get product information from multiple vendor websites. It worked….until it didn’t.
“The scraper worked.” That’s what I kept telling myself every time I opened the codebase. Five vendor websites, each with custom configurations, CSS selectors carefully mapped, API endpoints documented. Months of work. Production-ready :)
Until one morning, the pipeline failed. A vendor had redesigned their product page. One CSS class changed from .price-display to .product-price-main.
Four hours later, I pushed the fix.
A week after that, another vendor updated their API response structure. Another emergency fix. Another lost afternoon.
That’s when I realized: the most expensive code is the code that works just well enough to keep around.

The Illusion of “Done”
When I first built our scraper, I was proud of the architecture. Configuration-driven. Extensible. Each vendor got their own YAML file:
# Vendor A Configuration
website_config:
website_name: "Vendor A"
search_url: "www.vendor-a.com/search/?key="
parsing_method: "page"
webpages_config:
product_page:
identifiers:
css: ".product-container"
price_selector: ".price-display span"Multiply this by every vendor. Then maintain it forever.
The spider logic was clever — callbacks for search pages, callbacks for product pages, special handlers for edge cases. Around 450 lines of Python that knew exactly how to navigate each site.
It felt scalable. It felt professional. It felt done.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

“Every config is a liability waiting to happen.”The Hidden Tax Nobody Talks About
Here’s what the architecture diagrams don’t show you:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Over six months, I calculated that 40% of my scraping-related work was maintenance, not features.
The system worked. But it was a treadmill.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Victor Freitas on UnsplashEvery vendor website change meant developer time. Not ops time — developer time. The people who should be building new capabilities were instead playing whack-a-mole with CSS selectors.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Maintenance and Fixes: “This is the hidden tax.”
Tipping Point: When Five Became Fifty
The breaking point came when the business asked a simple question:
“Can we add 45 more vendor websites by Q3?”
I did the math:

• 45 websites × 3 hours average setup = 135 hours of configuration work

• Ongoing maintenance for 50 sites = permanent part-time job

• Every vendor redesign = pipeline risk
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Jackson Simmer on UnsplashThe rule-based approach that “worked” for 5 vendors would collapse at 50.
That’s when I started exploring a different paradigm entirely.
Enter the “Agentic” Mindset
The question that changed everything:
What if the system understood pages instead of following rules?
Traditional scrapers are like assembly line robots — precise, fast, but completely lost if you move a component 2 inches to the left.
Agentic systems are different. They perceive. They adapt. They reason.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

From rigid pipelines to adaptive intelligence.The New Architecture: Intelligence Over Configuration
Here’s what an agentic approach looks like in practice:
Instead of:
# 50+ lines of config per vendor
price_selector: ".price-display span"
search_url: "vendor.com/search/?q="
api_endpoint: "/api/v2/products"You get:
# Works on ANY website
result = await agent.get_price("PRODUCT-SKU-123", "any-vendor.com")That’s it. No configuration. The agent figures it out.
How? Three layers of intelligence:
1. Strategy Orchestration
The agent doesn’t just try one method — it has a playbook. Direct API call first (fastest). Browser automation second (handles JavaScript). Hybrid approach third (complex interactions). If one fails, it automatically falls back.
2. Perception
Using vision models and DOM analysis, the agent understands what’s on the page. It recognizes price patterns, identifies product information, detects obstacles like CAPTCHAs or login walls.
3. Adaptive Extraction
Heuristic patterns catch 80% of cases instantly. For complex pages, the LLM takes over — slower, but accurate. The system learns which approach works best for each domain.
Load Page → Analyze (DOM + Vision) → Price Found?
↓ Yes ↓ No
Extract & Return Try Actions (scroll, click, search)
↓
Max Retries? → Yes → Return with details
↓ No
Loop back to Analyze“The agent doesn’t just scrape — it reasons.”
The Results: What Changed
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The 450 lines of callback spaghetti became 150 lines of clean orchestration logic.
The config files? Deleted.
New vendor support? Instant. Point it at any e-commerce site and get a price. Yaaay!!!
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Bangun Stock Production on Unsplash
The Bigger Picture: Why Agentic Solutions Matter Now
This isn’t just a scraping story. It’s a pattern I’m seeing everywhere:
Rule-based systems scale linearly. Every new case requires new rules. More rules mean more maintenance. More maintenance means slower innovation.
Agentic systems scale logarithmically. The intelligence compounds. The same perception layer that understands Vendor A’s product page already knows how to handle Vendor B’s — even if it’s never seen it before.
We’re at an inflection point. The tools exist:

• Local LLMs (Ollama, llama.cpp) make AI inference accessible

• Vision models can understand screenshots like humans do

• Browser automation has matured (Playwright, anti-detection techniques)

• The “agent” paradigm gives us a mental model for orchestrating it all
The question isn’t whether to adopt agentic approaches — it’s where they’ll have the most impact in your stack.
What I Learned
1. “Working” can be a trap.
Code that works but drains resources is technical debt in disguise. The Scrapy scraper worked. It also consumed 40% of my time.
2. Configuration is a liability.
Every config file is a promise to maintain it forever. Zero-config isn’t laziness — it’s strategic.
3. Intelligence beats rules at scale.
When you can’t predict every edge case (and you never can), you need systems that adapt rather than systems that enumerate.
4. The future is agentic.
Not because it’s trendy — because it fundamentally changes the maintenance equation. You’re no longer writing rules for the world. You’re building systems that understand it.

Final Thought
I deleted 2,000 lines of “working” code. Removed entire directories. Watched the config files disappear from the repo.
It was terrifying.
And then it was freeing.
The new system handles more vendors with less code, adapts to changes I’ll never see, and lets me focus on problems that actually matter.
The most expensive code is the code that works just well enough to keep around.
Let it go! Build something that thinks.
